docs.title: pymizer
docs.text:
pymizer brings core mizer workflows into Python while continuing to rely on the mature R implementation for the actual model logic and numerical solvers.

The current wrapper focuses on the common interactive workflow:

  1. load or construct a MizerParams model
  2. run a projection with project()
  3. pull results back into Python as pandas, numpy, or xarray
import pymizer as mz

params = mz.new_community_params(no_w=20)
sim = params.project(t_max=5, dt=0.1, t_save=1, progress_bar=False)

biomass = sim.biomass()
times = sim.times()

docs.title: Why a Python wrapper?
docs.text:
mizer already has a rich R API and excellent package documentation. The goal of pymizer is not to replace that ecosystem, but to make it easier to:

  • use mizer from Python notebooks and analysis scripts
  • move simulation outputs directly into the Python data stack
  • access packaged example datasets from Python
  • keep the Python interface close to the mental model of the R package

docs.title: Current scope
docs.text:
The wrapper currently supports:

  • constructing models with new_multispecies_params(), new_single_species_params(), and new_community_params()
  • loading saved models with read_params()
  • running simulations through MizerParams.project()
  • accessing common outputs from MizerSim
  • listing and loading built-in mizer datasets

Advanced extension mechanisms in mizer, especially custom rate functions, are not yet wrapped in a Python-native way.

docs.title: Site Guide
docs.text:
• Get Started shows installation and the first simulation.
  • Articles provides worked examples.
  • Reference documents the Python API.
  • News summarises recent changes to the wrapper.

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
To use pymizer, you need:

  • Python 3.10 or later
  • R installed and available on your path
  • the mizer R package installed in your R library

docs.title: Install
docs.text:
For development from this repository:

python3 -m venv python/.venv
python/.venv/bin/pip install -e ./python

To install documentation tooling as well:

python/.venv/bin/pip install -e './python[docs]'

docs.title: First Simulation
docs.text:
The simplest way to confirm the bridge is working is to build a small community model and run a short projection:

import pymizer as mz

params = mz.new_community_params(no_w=20)
sim = params.project(
    t_max=5,
    dt=0.1,
    t_save=1,
    progress_bar=False,
)

print(sim.times())
print(sim.biomass())

Expected result:

  • sim.times() returns a NumPy array of saved years
  • sim.biomass() returns a labelled pandas.DataFrame

docs.title: Built-in Datasets
docs.text:
The wrapper can also load datasets shipped with the R package:

import pymizer as mz

print(mz.list_datasets())

species = mz.load_dataset("NS_species_params")
interaction = mz.load_dataset("NS_interaction")
params = mz.load_dataset("NS_params")
sim = mz.load_dataset("NS_sim")

Returned types depend on the dataset:

  • tabular datasets become pandas.DataFrame
  • NS_params becomes pymizer.MizerParams
  • NS_sim becomes pymizer.MizerSim

docs.title: Build The Docs
docs.text:
This website is built with MkDocs:

python/.venv/bin/mkdocs build -f python/mkdocs.yml

For live preview during editing:

python/.venv/bin/mkdocs serve -f python/mkdocs.yml

docs.title: Publish To GitHub Pages
docs.text:
pymizer is intended to publish its static documentation by pushing the built site to the repository's gh-pages branch from a local environment.

From the python/ directory:

mkdocs gh-deploy

This command builds the site and updates the gh-pages branch used by GitHub Pages.

• Initial pymizer proof-of-concept package added under python/
  • Added wrappers for MizerParams, MizerSim, model constructors, and simulation output accessors
  • Added built-in dataset access from Python
  • Added a MkDocs documentation site with articles and API reference

docs.title: Roadmap
docs.text:
This page tracks the planned development of pymizer, the Python wrapper for the R mizer package.

The current package already supports:

  • creating MizerParams wrappers from Python
  • running project() through the R mizer package
  • extracting common results as pandas, numpy, and xarray
  • loading built-in mizer datasets from Python
  • building a dedicated documentation website

The next stages should focus on turning the current proof of concept into a more robust and maintainable bridge for real analysis workflows.

docs.title: Guiding Principles
docs.text:
• Keep the Python API close to the mental model of mizer, but make return types feel natural in Python.
  • Prefer thin, reliable wrapping of stable R functionality over partial reimplementation in Python.
  • Expand the public API in layers, starting with the most common scientific workflows.
  • Treat packaging, testing, and documentation as product features, not polish.

docs.title: Goals
docs.text:
• Make the existing wrapper reliable for day-to-day use.
  • Reduce avoidable environment friction.
  • Tighten the boundaries of the supported API.

docs.title: Work Items
docs.text:
• Add explicit version checks for compatible R, mizer, and rpy2 versions.
  • Improve startup diagnostics when R or the mizer package is unavailable.
  • Standardise conversion helpers so all wrapped functions follow the same rules for pandas, numpy, and xarray outputs.
  • Add lightweight validation helpers for constructor inputs such as species_params and interaction matrices.
  • Improve object summaries so MizerParams.summary() and MizerSim reporting are more useful in notebooks.
  • Add convenience methods for common save/load workflows on MizerSim as well as MizerParams.

docs.title: Goals
docs.text:
• Cover the most commonly used read-only mizer analysis functions.
  • Make Python notebooks viable without frequent escapes into raw R objects.

docs.title: Priority Additions
docs.text:
• MizerSim accessors:
  • ssb()
  • yield_gear()
  • f_mort_gear()
  • pred_mort()
  • diet()
  • growth_curves()
  • MizerParams accessors:
  • initial_n()
  • initial_n_resource()
  • biomass()
  • feeding_level()
  • pred_rate()
  • indicator functions:
  • community slope
  • mean weight
  • proportion of large fish
  • trophic level outputs

docs.title: Phase 3: Parameter Editing And Model Manipulation
Goals
docs.text:
• Support the common “load, tweak, rerun” workflow from Python.
  • Preserve the immutability pattern used by mizer.

docs.title: Priority Additions
docs.text:
• set_interaction()
  • set_resource()
  • set_reproduction()
  • set_initial_values()
  • set_metadata()
  • set_pred_kernel()
  • set_search_volume()
  • set_max_intake_rate()
  • set_metabolic_rate()
  • project_to_steady() if feasible through the current bridge design

docs.title: Phase 4: Better Dataset And Example Support
Goals
docs.text: • Make the wrapper easy to learn through real models and reproducible examples.

docs.title: Work Items
docs.text:
• Add richer helpers around built-in datasets, such as loading matching North Sea inputs together.
  • Add example notebooks for:
  • community model
  • North Sea model
  • basic plotting and analysis in Python
  • Add a helper to expose package example file paths if needed.
  • Add a documented “recommended first workflow” that uses built-in example data only.

docs.title: Goals
docs.text:
• Make installation predictable outside this repository.
  • Clarify how Python and R dependencies should be managed together.

docs.title: Work Items
docs.text:
• Decide on the intended release model:
  • publish pymizer as a Python package only
  • or keep it as a repo-local companion package for now
  • Add wheel and source distribution checks.
  • Document installation strategies for:
  • local developer setup
  • virtualenv/venv
  • Conda or Mamba
  • CI environments
  • Decide whether installation should attempt any automatic R package checks or remain fully manual.
  • Add a minimal support matrix covering OS, Python version, and R version.

docs.title: Goals
docs.text: • Move from smoke tests to confidence-building coverage.

docs.title: Test Layers
docs.text:
• unit tests for conversion helpers and shape handling
  • integration tests for live R bridge behaviour
  • regression tests for built-in examples
  • documentation build tests

docs.title: Goals
docs.text: • Make output exploration feel more natural in the Python ecosystem.

docs.title: Possible Additions
docs.text:
• convenience plotting methods backed by matplotlib, seaborn, or plotly
  • helper methods for converting outputs to tidy long-form pandas
  • utility functions for common notebook display patterns
  • optional integration with Jupyter-rich summaries for wrapped objects

docs.title: Goals
docs.text: • Explore whether more of mizer’s extension surface can be exposed from Python without creating fragile abstractions.

docs.title: Candidates
docs.text:
• support for more extension hooks and metadata
  • controlled access to custom rate-function registration
  • pass-through helpers for advanced users who need raw R evaluation
  • helpers for serialising wrapper state in mixed Python/R workflows

docs.title: Recommended Near-Term Priorities
docs.text:
If development time is limited, the best next sequence is:

  1. Stabilise the bridge and input validation.
  2. Add the highest-value summary and indicator accessors.
  3. Expand tests and CI coverage.
  4. Publish example notebooks using the built-in datasets.
  5. Add selected parameter-setting methods for iterative modelling workflows.

docs.title: Milestone A: Usable Beta
docs.text:
• clean install process documented
  • constructor and projection workflows stable
  • built-in dataset loading stable
  • core result accessors covered by tests
  • docs site published

docs.title: Milestone B: Analysis-Ready
docs.text:
• common summary and indicator functions wrapped
  • North Sea example notebook available
  • CI runs bridge tests automatically
  • version compatibility policy documented

docs.title: Milestone C: Modelling-Ready
docs.text:
• important setter methods wrapped
  • save/load workflow broadened
  • more realistic end-to-end examples added
  • error handling improved for model editing workflows

docs.title: Source
docs.text: The canonical repository version of this plan also lives in the repository as python/ROADMAP.md.

docs.title: Community Model
docs.text: This example mirrors the lightest-weight path through the wrapper: create a community model in Python, run a projection in R through mizer, and bring the results back into Python objects.

docs.title: Create And Project
docs.text:
import pymizer as mz

params = mz.new_community_params(no_w=20)
sim = params.project(
    t_max=5,
    dt=0.1,
    t_save=1,
    progress_bar=False,
)

docs.title: Inspect Outputs
docs.text:
times = sim.times()
biomass = sim.biomass()
abundance = sim.abundance()
resource = sim.n_resource()

The returned objects are chosen to feel natural in Python:

  • times is a NumPy array
  • biomass and abundance are labelled pandas.DataFrame objects
  • resource is an xarray.DataArray by default

docs.title: Example Biomass Output
docs.text:
The smoke test for pymizer currently produces output shaped like this:

species     Community
time
0        34926.721959
1        46146.578715
2        50503.969482
3        52101.198790
4        52906.953622
5        53493.609873

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• The model construction and projection are still performed by the R mizer package.
  • The Python layer mainly handles conversion, object wrapping, and ergonomics.
  • When debugging environment problems, it is worth checking both Python and R package availability.

docs.title: Datasets
docs.text: The R package ships with several example datasets that are useful both for testing the wrapper and for learning the mizer workflow from Python.

docs.title: List Available Datasets
docs.text:
import pymizer as mz

datasets = mz.list_datasets()
print(datasets)

Typical entries include:

  • NS_species_params
  • NS_species_params_gears
  • NS_interaction
  • NS_params
  • NS_sim

docs.title: Load A Dataset
docs.text:
species = mz.load_dataset("NS_species_params")
interaction = mz.load_dataset("NS_interaction")
params = mz.load_dataset("NS_params")
sim = mz.load_dataset("NS_sim")

docs.title: Returned Python Types
docs.text:
load_dataset() maps dataset types to the closest Python representation:

  • R data.frame -> pandas.DataFrame
  • R matrix -> pandas.DataFrame with row and column labels
  • MizerParams -> pymizer.MizerParams
  • MizerSim -> pymizer.MizerSim

docs.title: North Sea Example
docs.text:
You can rebuild the North Sea parameter object from Python inputs:

species = mz.load_dataset("NS_species_params")
interaction = mz.load_dataset("NS_interaction")

params = mz.new_multispecies_params(
    species_params=species,
    interaction=interaction,
)

This is often the most convenient starting point for notebooks because the inputs are already in pandas.

docs.title: Reference
docs.text: The pymizer reference is organised into a few small modules.

docs.title: Main API
docs.text:
• Model API: model constructors and wrapper classes
  • Dataset API: list and load packaged example datasets
  • R Bridge API: lower-level access to the embedded R environment

docs.title: Design Notes
docs.text:
The public API deliberately stays narrower than the full R package:

  • constructor functions are exposed at top level
  • MizerParams and MizerSim provide the most common workflows
  • raw R objects remain accessible through the .r property when needed

docs.title: MizerError
docs.text:
Bases: RuntimeError

Raised when the R bridge cannot complete an operation.

docs.title: MizerREnvironment(package_name='mizer') dataclass
docs.text: Thin wrapper around the embedded R session used by pymizer.

docs.title: call(name, *args, **kwargs)
docs.text: Call an exported R function from the mizer package.

docs.title: save_rds(obj, path)
docs.text: Save an R object to an RDS file.

docs.title: read_rds(path)
docs.text: Read an RDS file.

docs.title: class_name(obj)
docs.text: Return the first class name for an R object.

docs.title: is_s4(obj)
docs.text: Return whether an object is an S4 object.

docs.title: slot_names(obj)
docs.text: Return slot names for an S4 object.

docs.title: dataframe_from_r(obj)
docs.text: Convert an R object to a pandas DataFrame via as.data.frame().

docs.title: get_environment()
docs.text: Return the singleton R environment used by pymizer.

docs.title: list_datasets(env=None)
docs.text: List datasets shipped with the R mizer package.

docs.title: load_dataset(name, env=None)
docs.text: Load a built-in mizer dataset and convert it to a natural Python type.

docs.title: MizerParams(_r_obj, _env) dataclass
docs.text: Python wrapper around an R MizerParams object.

docs.title: r property
docs.text: Access the underlying R object.

docs.title: copy()
docs.text: Return another wrapper for the same underlying object.

docs.title: project(effort=None, *, t_max=100, dt=0.1, t_save=1, t_start=0, progress_bar=False, **kwargs)
docs.text: Run mizer::project() and wrap the resulting MizerSim.

docs.title: set_fishing(**kwargs)
docs.text: Call setFishing() and wrap the returned params object.

docs.title: save(path)
docs.text: Save the params object using saveParams().

docs.title: summary()
docs.text: Return the text representation of summary(params).

docs.title: MizerSim(_r_obj, _env) dataclass
docs.text: Python wrapper around an R MizerSim object.

docs.title: r property
docs.text: Access the underlying R object.

docs.title: params()
docs.text: Return the MizerParams used to create the simulation.

docs.title: times()
docs.text: Return saved times as a numpy array.

docs.title: biomass()
docs.text: Return biomass through time as a pandas DataFrame.

docs.title: abundance()
docs.text: Return total abundance through time as a pandas DataFrame.

docs.title: yield_()
docs.text: Return fisheries yield through time as a pandas DataFrame.

docs.title: n(*, as_xarray=True)
docs.text: Return the species abundance array.

docs.title: n_resource(*, as_xarray=True)
docs.text: Return the resource abundance array.

docs.title: f_mort(*, as_xarray=True)
docs.text: Return fishing mortality through time.

docs.title: feeding_level(*, as_xarray=True)
docs.text: Return feeding levels through time.

docs.title: new_multispecies_params(*, species_params, interaction=None, env=None, **kwargs)
docs.text: Wrap newMultispeciesParams() with pandas-friendly inputs.

docs.title: new_single_species_params(*, species_params, env=None, **kwargs)
docs.text: Wrap newSingleSpeciesParams().

docs.title: new_community_params(env=None, **kwargs)
docs.text: Wrap newCommunityParams().

docs.title: read_params(path, env=None)
docs.text: Load a saved params object with readParams().
